content.text: Developer Docs and Runbooks in the Portal

Embedding documentation directly into the Internal Developer Portal (IDP) transforms docs from an afterthought into a first-class part of the platform. When architecture diagrams, API references, runbooks, and onboarding guides live alongside the service catalog entries they describe, developers find the context they need without switching tools — and documentation stays accurate because it is colocated with the code it documents and validated in the same CI pipeline.

This sub-subtopic covers how to structure, publish, and maintain docs for GenAI components using Backstage TechDocs, and how to author runbooks that reduce mean time to resolution for GenAI-specific incidents.

attrs.alt: Developer docs and runbooks architecture in the IDP

content.text: Why the IDP Is the Right Home for GenAI Documentation

GenAI services have richer operational context than typical microservices. An LLM inference service exposes not just an HTTP endpoint but also a prompt contract, model version history, eval thresholds, token budget configuration, and a rollback procedure that differs from a standard deployment rollback. Keeping this context scattered across Confluence, GitHub wikis, and Notion creates maintenance burden and on-call confusion.
Documentation type
Without IDP
With IDP
Runbooks
Confluence, outdated, hard to find during incidents
Linked from catalog entry, versioned in Git, surfaced in Backstage
API reference
PDF or Postman collection emailed around
Auto-generated from OpenAPI spec, always matches deployed version
Architecture ADRs
Google Doc, unlinked from service
docs/adr/ in repo, rendered via TechDocs next to service entry
Onboarding guides
Wiki page last updated 18 months ago
Validated as part of CI; failing links break the build
Model version history
Spreadsheet or Notion database
CHANGELOG.md in model repo, linked from catalog entry
TechDocs: Backstage's Docs-as-Code Engine
How TechDocs Works

TechDocs converts Markdown files (organised with MkDocs) into static HTML and stores the rendered output in an object-storage bucket (GCS or S3). Backstage fetches and displays the HTML in its UI. The key property is that the documentation source lives in the same Git repository as the service code — there is no separate CMS to maintain.
Developer writes Markdown in docs/ inside the service repository
CI pipeline runs mkdocs build --strict and pushes output to the TechDocs storage bucket
Backstage reads the catalog-info.yaml annotation pointing to the rendered docs
Developer browses docs inside the Backstage UI, linked directly from the service catalog entry
mkdocs.yml for a GenAI Service

# mkdocs.yml  (repo root)
site_name: LLM Gateway
site_description: Internal LLM routing and policy enforcement service
repo_url: https://github.com/myorg/llm-gateway
docs_dir: docs/
strict: true          # fail on warnings — enforced in CI

nav:
  - Overview: index.md
  - Architecture:
      - System diagram: architecture/system.md
      - Data flow: architecture/data-flow.md
      - ADRs: architecture/adrs/
  - API Reference:
      - Endpoints: api/endpoints.md
      - Authentication: api/auth.md
      - Rate limits: api/rate-limits.md
      - Error codes: api/errors.md
  - Runbooks:
      - Model rollback: runbooks/model-rollback.md
      - Cost spike triage: runbooks/cost-spike.md
      - Hallucination escalation: runbooks/hallucination.md
      - Prompt regression: runbooks/prompt-regression.md
  - Onboarding: onboarding.md
  - Changelog: changelog.md

plugins:
  - techdocs-core     # Backstage-required plugin
  - search
  - git-revision-date-localized:
      enable_creation_date: true

markdown_extensions:
  - admonition
  - pymdownx.superfences
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true
  - tables
  - toc:
      permalink: true
Linking Docs to the Catalog Entry
The
catalog-info.yaml
annotation tells Backstage where to fetch the rendered TechDocs output. Without this annotation the service entry has no docs tab.

# catalog-info.yaml
apiVersion: backstage.io/v1alpha1
kind: Component
metadata:
  name: llm-gateway
  annotations:
    backstage.io/techdocs-ref: dir:.    # docs live in this repo's docs/ dir
    github.com/project-slug: myorg/llm-gateway
spec:
  type: service
  owner: platform-team
  lifecycle: production
Authoring GenAI Runbooks That Actually Help On-Call

Runbooks for GenAI services differ from standard service runbooks because the failure modes include prompt regressions, model quality degradation, eval threshold violations, and unexpected token cost spikes — all of which require different diagnostic steps than a typical 5xx surge.
Runbook Template Structure
Every runbook must answer five questions without requiring the on-call engineer to chase down additional context:
Section
Contents
Symptom
Alert name, what the user sees, dashboard link
Severity
P1/P2/P3, SLA impact, who must be paged
Diagnostic steps
Numbered commands with expected output for each step
Likely causes
Ordered by frequency; each cause links to its fix
Escalation
When to escalate, who to page, rollback decision threshold
Example: Model Rollback Runbook

# Model Rollback: LLM Gateway

## Symptom
Alert: `llm_gateway_eval_pass_rate_below_threshold`
User impact: Response quality degraded; hallucination rate above baseline.
Dashboard: https://grafana.internal/d/llm-gateway-evals

## Severity
P2 — quality SLA breach. Page on-call platform engineer if not resolved in 30 min.

## Diagnostic Steps

1. Identify the failing model version:
   ```
   kubectl get configmap llm-gateway-routing -o jsonpath='{.data.model_versions}'
   ```
   Expected: JSON map of model aliases to version tags.

2. Check eval scores for the current version:
   ```
   curl -s https://llm-gateway.internal/api/v1/evals/latest | jq '.scores'
   ```
   Expected: `pass_rate >= 0.95`. If below, proceed to rollback.

3. Verify the previous stable version tag:
   ```
   cat deployments/model-versions.yaml | grep -A2 'stable-previous'
   ```

## Rollback Procedure

1. Update the routing config to the previous stable version:
   ```
   kubectl patch configmap llm-gateway-routing \
     --patch '{"data":{"active_model":"gpt-4o-2024-05-13"}}'
   ```

2. Trigger a re-eval to confirm improvement:
   ```
   curl -X POST https://llm-gateway.internal/api/v1/evals/run \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $EVAL_TOKEN" \
     -d '{"suite":"smoke","model":"gpt-4o-2024-05-13"}'
   ```
   Wait ~5 min for results. Confirm `pass_rate >= 0.95`.

3. File a model regression incident ticket with:
   - Failing version tag
   - Eval scores before and after
   - Link to the model changelog diff

## Escalation
If rollback does not restore eval scores within 30 minutes, page the GenAI
model owner and open a P1 incident. Do NOT roll forward — hold the previous
stable version until root cause is confirmed.
Example: Cost Spike Triage Runbook

# Cost Spike Triage: LLM Gateway

## Symptom
Alert: `llm_gateway_daily_token_spend_exceeds_budget_150pct`
User impact: None immediately, but budget exhaustion blocks all LLM calls.

## Diagnostic Steps

1. Identify the top-spending teams in the last hour:
   ```
   curl -s "https://llm-gateway.internal/api/v1/usage/top?window=1h&limit=5" | jq '.'
   ```

2. Find the request pattern driving the spike:
   ```
   # In Grafana, run:
   sum by (team, model) (rate(llm_gateway_tokens_total[5m]))
   ```

3. Check if a new service version was deployed in the last 2 hours:
   ```
   kubectl rollout history deployment/llm-gateway --namespace genai-prod
   ```

## Likely Causes (by frequency)

1. **Runaway evaluation loop** — an eval job sending thousands of requests
   Fix: `kubectl delete job -l job-type=eval -n genai-prod`

2. **Missing token limit on new environment** — sandbox provisioned without budget
   Fix: Patch gateway credential via `platform/provisioner/llm_gateway.py`

3. **Prompt expansion bug** — context window stuffed with irrelevant retrieved chunks
   Fix: Roll back the offending service version; file prompt regression bug

## Escalation
If spend exceeds 200% budget, throttle the top-spending team immediately:
```
curl -X PATCH https://llm-gateway.internal/api/v1/credentials/{team_id} \
  -d '{"limits":{"tokens_per_day":0}}'
```
Notify team lead via Slack before throttling.
CI Validation for Documentation
Documentation quality must be enforced in CI, not left to voluntary reviews. Three categories of checks run on every PR touching the
docs/
directory:

# .github/workflows/docs-validate.yml
name: Docs Validation
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['docs/**', 'mkdocs.yml', 'catalog-info.yaml']

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install mkdocs mkdocs-techdocs-core mkdocs-git-revision-date-localized

      - name: Build docs (strict mode)
        run: mkdocs build --strict
        # --strict: warnings become errors; broken internal links fail the build

      - name: Check runbook completeness
        run: |
          python3 scripts/check-runbooks.py docs/runbooks/
        # Verifies each runbook has: Symptom, Severity, Diagnostic Steps, Escalation sections

      - name: Validate catalog annotation
        run: |
          python3 -c "
          import yaml
          with open('catalog-info.yaml') as f:
              entry = yaml.safe_load(f)
          annotations = entry['metadata'].get('annotations', {})
          assert 'backstage.io/techdocs-ref' in annotations, \
              'catalog-info.yaml must include backstage.io/techdocs-ref annotation'
          print('catalog annotation OK')
          "
The runbook completeness checker is a small Python script that parses each runbook's Markdown headings and fails if any required section is missing:
Documentation Taxonomy for GenAI Components

Platform teams should agree on a standard folder structure for GenAI service documentation so that on-call engineers can navigate any service's docs without needing to learn each team's individual layout.

docs/
├── index.md                    # One-paragraph overview + architecture diagram
├── architecture/
│   ├── system.md               # Component diagram, external dependencies
│   ├── data-flow.md            # Request path, data classification, PII handling
│   └── adrs/
│       ├── 001-model-routing.md
│       └── 002-eval-framework.md
├── api/
│   ├── endpoints.md            # Generated from OpenAPI spec
│   ├── auth.md                 # Authentication and credential lifecycle
│   ├── rate-limits.md          # Per-tier limits, token budgets
│   └── errors.md               # Error codes with remediation steps
├── runbooks/
│   ├── model-rollback.md       # Roll back to previous stable model version
│   ├── cost-spike.md           # Triage unexpected token spend
│   ├── hallucination.md        # Escalation SOP for quality degradation
│   └── prompt-regression.md    # Debug and revert prompt regressions
├── onboarding.md               # New team checklist: access, tooling, first eval run
└── changelog.md                # Semantic version history with model version notes
Keeping Docs in Sync with GenAI-Specific Changes
Model Version Changelogs

GenAI services often change behavior without a code deployment: when the underlying model is upgraded, prompt behavior can shift even if no application code changes. The changelog must capture model version changes explicitly, not just code versions.

# Changelog: LLM Gateway

## [2.4.0] — 2026-06-15
### Changed
- Upgraded default model alias `gpt-4o` → `gpt-4o-2026-05-13`
  - Eval pass rate: 97.2% (was 96.8%)
  - P95 latency: 1.4s (was 1.6s)
  - Token cost: +3% (updated budget configs in terraform/environments/)
- Updated prompt contract: system prompt now includes `output_format: json_strict`
  — downstream consumers that parse raw text MUST be updated

### Fixed
- Hallucination rate on multi-turn conversations reduced from 2.1% to 0.9%
  after adding conversation truncation at 12k tokens

## [2.3.1] — 2026-05-28
### Fixed
- Rate limit headers now correctly reflect per-team quota (was returning global limit)
Auto-Generating API Reference from OpenAPI

Manually authored API docs drift from the actual implementation within weeks. The remedy is to generate the API reference automatically from the OpenAPI specification served by the gateway and embed the result into the TechDocs build:

# In CI, before mkdocs build:
- name: Generate API reference from OpenAPI spec
  run: |
    # Fetch live spec from staging gateway
    curl -s https://llm-gateway-staging.internal/openapi.json > /tmp/openapi.json

    # Convert to Markdown using openapi-generator-cli
    docker run --rm \
      -v /tmp/openapi.json:/openapi.json \
      -v $(pwd)/docs/api:/output \
      openapitools/openapi-generator-cli generate \
        --input-spec /openapi.json \
        --generator-name markdown \
        --output /output

    git diff --exit-code docs/api/ || \
      echo "::warning::API docs drifted from spec — review generated diff"
Surfacing Runbooks from Alerts

Runbooks are only useful if on-call engineers can find them during an incident. The most effective linking strategy is to embed the runbook URL directly in the Prometheus alert rule so it appears in the Alertmanager notification:

# prometheus/alerts/llm-gateway.yaml
groups:
  - name: llm-gateway
    rules:
      - alert: LLMGatewayEvalPassRateBelowThreshold
        expr: |
          llm_gateway_eval_pass_rate{suite="production"} < 0.95
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: p2
          team: platform
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM Gateway eval pass rate below 95%"
          description: >
            Model {{ $labels.model }} pass rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}.
            Current routing config: check configmap llm-gateway-routing.
          runbook_url: >
            https://backstage.internal/catalog/default/component/llm-gateway/docs/runbooks/model-rollback
          dashboard_url: >
            https://grafana.internal/d/llm-gateway-evals?var-model={{ $labels.model }}

      - alert: LLMGatewayDailySpendExceedsBudget
        expr: |
          llm_gateway_daily_token_spend_usd > llm_gateway_daily_budget_usd * 1.5
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: p2
          team: platform
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM Gateway token spend exceeds 150% of daily budget"
          runbook_url: >
            https://backstage.internal/catalog/default/component/llm-gateway/docs/runbooks/cost-spike
Summary
Hosting developer docs and runbooks in the IDP closes the gap between service metadata and operational knowledge. The key practices for GenAI services are:
Docs-as-code with TechDocs: Markdown in Git, rendered by CI into Backstage — no separate CMS
Standard folder layout: architecture/, api/, runbooks/, onboarding.md, changelog.md — every service follows the same map
GenAI runbooks include model rollback, cost spike triage, hallucination escalation, and prompt regression procedures
CI validates runbook completeness (required sections) and docs build (strict mode) on every PR
Model version changelogs explicitly note model upgrades and prompt contract changes, not just code versions
Alert annotations embed direct runbook URLs so on-call engineers reach the right procedure immediately

Together these practices ensure documentation remains accurate, discoverable, and useful under the pressure of a real incident — when good runbooks reduce the gap between alert and resolution from hours to minutes.
